paper_map.title: Alloy Design, Combinatorial Synthesis, and Microstructure–Property Relations for Low-Density Fe-Mn-Al-C Austenitic Steels
paper_map.abstract_summary: The paper reviews low-density austenitic steels based on the Fe-Mn-Al-C system with up to 18% reduced mass density. Two steel types are highlighted: single-phase austenitic TWIP (SIMPLEX) steels containing 25–30 wt.% Mn and <4–5 wt.% Al (or <8 wt.% Al when naturally aged), and κ-carbide strengthened austenitic steels with higher Al content where κ-carbides form at 500–600°C or during quenching. The work addresses combinatorial bulk high-throughput design, microstructure–property relations, and hydrogen embrittlement susceptibility.
paper_map.research_objective: To study alloy design, high-throughput synthesis, and microstructure–property relationships of low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels, including TWIP and κ-carbide strengthened variants, and their response to hydrogen.
material_systems: Low-density TWIP / SIMPLEX steel (25–30 wt.% Mn, <4–5 wt.% Al; also aged with <8 wt.% Al)
κ-carbide strengthened austenitic steel (higher Al, >10 wt.% Al, with κ-carbides formed at 500–600°C or during quenching)
Ferritic low-density steels (Mn <8 wt.%, Al 5–8 wt.%, C <0.3 wt.%)
Austenite–ferrite complex grades (Mn 5–30 wt.%, Al 3–10 wt.%, C 0.1–0.7 wt.%)
main_process_variables: Thin-strip casting with in-line hot rolling
key_sections: Bulk combinatorial design of low-density austenitic steels
Microstructure–property relations for low-density austenitic steels
The effect of hydrogen on the microstructure and mechanical behavior of low-density austenitic steels
